text: Upon entering the chest, a number of anterior adhesions are taken down using electrocautery.
Due to the posterior attachment, an anterior inspection is first undertaken.
Here, the middle lobe vein is identified to be originating from the inferior vein.
The upper lobe vein, which has been identified and encircled, is divided using a vascular staple load.
The superior thoracoscopic visualization allows identification of this abnormal vein draining directly into the superior vena cava, which is likewise encircled and divided.
The pulmonary artery is now inspected.
The truncus anterior is identified.
Care must be taken during this dissection due to the posterior fixation of this tumor.
Now the upper lobe bronchus is identified and encircled and divided using a stapler.
It is helpful in a case like this where retraction is limited to use bronchoscopic confirmation before division.
Now the fissure is completed in standard manner using sequential firing of the endo-GIA stapler.
The completed dissection reveals the fully mobilized upper lobe now attached only to the chest wall.
The chest wall resection is now undertaken.
The margins are defined internally using thoracoscopic visualization and guidance.
A right angle clamp is palpated externally and this helps to define the margins of the excision externally.
The counter incision is now made.
Note the standard thoracotomy marked in marking pen and how this incision is slightly different to that which would normally be made.
With excellent visualization both from outside and using the thoracoscope from inside the chest, ribs two, three, and four are divided anteriorly using rib shears.
The posterior extent of this tumor requires disarticulation of the facet joints posteriorly.
This is accomplished using an osteotome, again, under dual visualization.
The specimen is retrieved on block and sent to pathology for examination of the margins.
Standard lymph node dissection is undertaken.
In this case, the chest wall is reconstructed using a Gore-Tex patch secured in place using interrupted proline sutures.
A single 24 French chest tube is exteriorized through the camera incision and the chest is closed.
A modified lateral decubitus position is used to gain access to the anterior chest wall.
Anterior attachment facilitates the posterior dissection first with identification of the bronchus to the upper lobe after retrieval of the level 11 lymph node.
This bronchus, once encircled, is again divided using a stapler with bronchoscopic confirmation prior to division.
Anterior fixation limits the amount of dissection which can be performed at this time.
However, the pleura overlying the superior pulmonary vein is opened with some exposure.
Here, the tumor is seen attached to the chest wall.
And again, as in the previous case, the internal margins are identified and marked using bovie electrocautery.
A right angle clamp is used to allow external palpation and planning of the external counter incision.
Once this is done, the ribs are again divided under dual visualization.
Here is the view through the counter incision.
Note the retractor is only in the soft tissue.

Once the two ribs have been divided, it is possible to completely mobilize the upper lobe, allowing for division of the superior pulmonary vein, retrieval of the level 11 lymph node, allowing for identification of the truncus anterior of the pulmonary artery, which is divided using an endo-GIA stapler with a vascular load.
The fissure is again completed in standard manner, completing the case.
